expanded_description: Understanding the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory that predicts molecular geometry based on the repulsion between electron pairs around a central atom, including bonding pairs and lone pairs.

core_concept.principle: VSEPR theory states that electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) around a central atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion, determining the molecular geometry.
core_concept.explanation: The Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory provides a simple model for predicting molecular shapes. Electron pairs in the valence shell of a central atom repel each other due to their negative charges. To minimize this repulsion, electron pairs adopt arrangements that maximize their separation. The resulting electron pair geometry, combined with the distinction between bonding pairs and lone pairs, determines the observed molecular geometry. Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, causing bond angles to deviate from ideal values.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron pairs around a central atom arranging to maximize separation
image.caption: Electron pairs around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion, determining molecular geometry

sections.introduction: Molecular geometry profoundly affects chemical properties including polarity, reactivity, and biological activity. VSEPR theory provides a straightforward method to predict molecular shapes from Lewis structures without complex quantum mechanical calculations.

key_concept.definition: VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry by assuming that electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) in the valence shell of a central atom repel each other and arrange themselves to maximize separation.
key_concept.context: This theory applies to main group elements and works best when the central atom follows the octet rule, though it can be extended to expanded octets.

principles.statement: Electron pairs around a central atom repel each other due to their negative charges and adopt arrangements that minimize repulsion.
principles.application: Predicts electron pair geometry based on total number of electron pairs (bonding + lone pairs).
principles.example: 4 electron pairs → tetrahedral arrangement (109.5° bond angles); 3 electron pairs → trigonal planar (120°)
principles.analogy: Like balloons tied together at a central point: they naturally spread apart to maximize distance between them.

principles.statement: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs because they are closer to the central atom and occupy more space.
principles.application: Explains why bond angles decrease when lone pairs are present; lone pairs compress bonding pair angles.
principles.example: CH₄ (0 lone pairs): 109.5°; NH₃ (1 lone pair): 107°; H₂O (2 lone pairs): 104.5°
principles.analogy: Like a large person (lone pair) sitting at a table takes up more space than a small person (bonding pair), pushing others closer together.

principles.statement: Multiple bonds (double, triple) exert greater repulsion than single bonds due to higher electron density.
principles.application: Explains bond angle variations in molecules with multiple bonds; multiple bonds treated as single electron domains for geometry.
principles.example: In H₂CO, the C=O double bond exerts more repulsion than C-H single bonds, affecting bond angles
principles.analogy: Like a thicker rope (double bond) takes up more space than a thinner rope (single bond) when bundled together.

classifications.value: Arrangement of all electron pairs (bonding + lone pairs) around central atom
characteristics: Based on total number of electron domains
Includes both bonding and lone pairs
Ideal angles: 2 domains (180°), 3 domains (120°), 4 domains (109.5°), 5 domains (90°, 120°), 6 domains (90°)
classifications.behavior: Electron domains arrange to maximize separation regardless of whether they are bonding or lone pairs

examples.explanation: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair → tetrahedral electron domain geometry (but different molecular geometry)
2 bonding pairs, 2 lone pairs → tetrahedral electron domain geometry (but different molecular geometry)

classifications.value: Arrangement of atoms (not lone pairs) around central atom
characteristics: Based on positions of atoms only (lone pairs invisible)
May differ from electron domain geometry when lone pairs present
classifications.behavior: Molecular geometry is what we observe experimentally; lone pairs influence shape but are not part of the geometry name

examples.explanation: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → tetrahedral molecular geometry (same as electron domain)
3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry (different from tetrahedral electron domain)
2 bonding pairs, 2 lone pairs → bent molecular geometry (different from tetrahedral electron domain)

classifications.value: Different types of electron pairs exert different repulsion strengths
characteristics: Lone pair-lone pair (LP-LP) > Lone pair-bonding pair (LP-BP) > Bonding pair-bonding pair (BP-BP)
Multiple bonds > Single bonds in repulsion
Explains bond angle deviations from ideal values
classifications.behavior: Stronger repulsions compress bond angles; lone pairs cause greater angle compression than bonding pairs

examples.explanation: H₂O has 2 lone pairs with strong LP-LP repulsion; bond angle compressed from 109.5° to 104.5°
NH₃ has 1 lone pair with LP-BP repulsion; bond angle compressed from 109.5° to 107°

worked_examples.title: Determining Electron Domain Geometry
worked_examples.problem: Determine the electron domain geometry for a molecule with 4 electron domains around the central atom.

step_by_step.explanation: 4 electron domains (bonding pairs + lone pairs)
4 electron domains adopt tetrahedral arrangement to maximize separation
Tetrahedral arrangement has ideal bond angles of 109.5°
Electron domain geometry is tetrahedral regardless of whether domains are bonding or lone pairs

worked_examples.final_answer: 4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry with 109.5° ideal bond angles
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron domain geometry depends only on total number of electron domains, not whether they are bonding or lone pairs.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Bond Angle Deviations
worked_examples.problem: Explain why the H-N-H bond angle in NH₃ (107°) is smaller than the H-C-H bond angle in CH₄ (109.5°).

step_by_step.step: Determine electron domain geometry for both
step_by_step.explanation: Both CH₄ and NH₃ have 4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
CH₄: 0 lone pairs; NH₃: 1 lone pair
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄: 4 BP, 0 LP; NH₃: 3 BP, 1 LP

step_by_step.explanation: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs (LP-BP > BP-BP)
step_by_step.calculation: Lone pair compresses bonding pair angles

step_by_step.explanation: Lone pair in NH₃ pushes bonding pairs closer together, reducing bond angle from ideal 109.5° to 107°

worked_examples.final_answer: NH₃ has 1 lone pair that exerts greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing the H-N-H bond angle from 109.5° to 107°; CH₄ has no lone pairs so maintains ideal tetrahedral angle
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, causing bond angle compression from ideal values.

sections.key_concept: The steric number (total number of electron domains) determines the electron domain geometry. Each single bond, multiple bond, or lone pair counts as one electron domain.

categories.description: Each bonding region (single, double, or triple bond) counts as one electron domain; each lone pair counts as one electron domain.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Multiple bonds are treated as single electron domains because all electrons in a multiple bond occupy the same region of space around the central atom.

examples.explanation: Central C has 2 double bonds; each double bond = 1 domain; total = 2 electron domains
Central C has 2 single bonds + 1 double bond; total = 3 electron domains
Central N has 3 single bonds + 1 lone pair; total = 4 electron domains

categories.description: Steric number (SN) = number of atoms bonded to central atom + number of lone pairs on central atom.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Steric number equals total electron domains; determines electron domain geometry according to VSEPR theory.

examples.explanation: Linear electron domain geometry (180°); examples: CO₂, BeCl₂
Trigonal planar electron domain geometry (120°); examples: BF₃, SO₃
Tetrahedral electron domain geometry (109.5°); examples: CH₄, NH₃, H₂O
Trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry (90°, 120°); examples: PCl₅, SF₄
Octahedral electron domain geometry (90°); examples: SF₆, BrF₅

categories.description: VSEPR predictions begin with a correct Lewis structure showing all bonding and lone pairs.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Draw Lewis structure first, count electron domains on central atom, determine electron domain geometry, then determine molecular geometry based on atom positions.

examples.explanation: Show all valence electrons, bonds, and lone pairs
Count bonding regions + lone pairs on central atom
Based on total electron domains
Based on positions of atoms only (ignore lone pairs for geometry name)

classifications.value: Linear electron domain geometry with 180° bond angles
characteristics: 2 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → linear molecular geometry
classifications.behavior: Electron domains arrange on opposite sides of central atom for maximum separation

examples.explanation: O=C=O; 2 double bonds = 2 domains; linear geometry; 180° bond angle
Cl-Be-Cl; 2 single bonds = 2 domains; linear geometry; 180° bond angle

classifications.value: Trigonal planar electron domain geometry with 120° bond angles
characteristics: 3 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar molecular geometry
2 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair → bent molecular geometry
Lone pair causes slight angle compression
classifications.behavior: Electron domains arrange in plane with 120° separation; lone pair reduces bond angles slightly

examples.explanation: 3 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs; trigonal planar; 120° bond angles
2 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair; bent molecular geometry; <120° bond angle

classifications.value: Tetrahedral electron domain geometry with 109.5° ideal bond angles
characteristics: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → tetrahedral molecular geometry
3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal
Lone pairs progressively compress bond angles
classifications.behavior: Most common geometry for main group elements; lone pairs cause significant angle compression

examples.explanation: 4 BP, 0 LP; tetrahedral; 109.5° bond angles
3 BP, 1 LP; trigonal pyramidal; 107° bond angles
2 BP, 2 LP; bent; 104.5° bond angles

worked_examples.title: Determining Steric Number from Lewis Structure
worked_examples.problem: Determine the steric number and electron domain geometry for the central atom in SF₄.

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; 4 F atoms contribute 4 electrons for bonding; S has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 lone pair

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains adopt trigonal bipyramidal arrangement
Trigonal bipyramidal has 90° (axial-equatorial) and 120° (equatorial-equatorial) angles

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₄: Steric number = 5; Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Bond angles = 90° and 120°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number = bonding pairs + lone pairs; determines electron domain geometry.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Molecular Geometry from Electron Domains
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry of XeF₂ given that Xe has 2 bonding pairs and 3 lone pairs.

step_by_step.explanation: 2 bonding pairs + 3 lone pairs = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains → trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry

step_by_step.step: Position lone pairs for minimum repulsion
step_by_step.explanation: Lone pairs occupy equatorial positions (120° apart) to minimize LP-LP repulsion
Only atoms (not lone pairs) determine molecular geometry; 2 F atoms in axial positions
F-Xe-F bond angle is 180° (axial positions)

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₂: Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = linear; Bond angle = 180°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize repulsion; molecular geometry based on atom positions only.

sections.key_concept: Each steric number corresponds to a specific electron domain geometry, with multiple possible molecular geometries depending on the number of lone pairs present.

process_flow.description: Count bonding pairs + lone pairs on central atom.
Match steric number to electron domain geometry (linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, octahedral).
Determine number of lone pairs on central atom.
process_flow.result: Number of lone pairs (0 to SN-2)

process_flow.description: Based on atom positions only; lone pairs influence shape but not named in geometry.
Start with ideal angles for electron domain geometry; adjust for lone pair repulsion.

classifications.behavior: All SN = 2 molecules are linear regardless of bonding type

examples.explanation: O=C=O; 2 double bonds; linear; 180°
H-C≡N; 2 bonding regions; linear; 180°

classifications.value: Two molecular geometries possible depending on lone pairs
classifications.behavior: Lone pair compresses bond angles below ideal 120°

examples.explanation: 3 BP, 0 LP; trigonal planar; 120°
2 BP, 1 LP; bent; ~119°

classifications.value: Three molecular geometries possible depending on lone pairs
classifications.behavior: Each lone pair compresses bond angles by ~2-2.5°

examples.explanation: 4 BP, 0 LP; tetrahedral; 109.5°
3 BP, 1 LP; trigonal pyramidal; 107°
2 BP, 2 LP; bent; 104.5°

classifications.value: Four molecular geometries possible depending on lone pairs
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs occupy equatorial positions to minimize 90° interactions

examples.explanation: 5 BP, 0 LP; trigonal bipyramidal
4 BP, 1 LP; seesaw
3 BP, 2 LP; T-shaped
2 BP, 3 LP; linear

classifications.value: Three molecular geometries possible depending on lone pairs
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions to minimize LP-LP repulsion

examples.explanation: 6 BP, 0 LP; octahedral; 90°
5 BP, 1 LP; square pyramidal; <90°
4 BP, 2 LP; square planar; 90°

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for SN = 4 Molecules
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for H₃O⁺ (hydronium ion).

step_by_step.explanation: O is central atom with 6 valence electrons; 3 H atoms bond; +1 charge means 1 fewer electron; O has 1 lone pair
step_by_step.calculation: O: 6 valence e⁻ - 1 (charge) = 5 e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 LP

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 4 electron domains
4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
3 atoms + 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry
1 lone pair compresses angles from 109.5° to ~107° (similar to NH₃)

worked_examples.final_answer: H₃O⁺: Steric number = 4; Electron domain geometry = tetrahedral; Molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal; Bond angle ≈ 107°
worked_examples.concept_applied: H₃O⁺ is isoelectronic with NH₃; same electron domain count gives same molecular geometry.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for SN = 5 Molecules
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for ClF₃ (chlorine trifluoride).

step_by_step.explanation: Cl is central atom with 7 valence electrons; 3 F atoms bond; Cl has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Cl: 7 valence e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 LP

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains → trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry
2 lone pairs occupy equatorial positions to minimize LP-LP repulsion (120° apart)
3 atoms in T-shape (2 axial + 1 equatorial)
Lone pairs compress angles below 90°

worked_examples.final_answer: ClF₃: Steric number = 5; Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = T-shaped; Bond angle <90°
worked_examples.concept_applied: For SN = 5, lone pairs occupy equatorial positions; 2 lone pairs give T-shaped molecular geometry.

sections.section_title: Lone Pair Effects on Molecular Geometry
sections.key_concept: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, causing systematic deviations from ideal bond angles and influencing which positions lone pairs occupy in molecules with steric numbers 5 and 6.

components.definition: Lone pairs are nonbonding electron pairs on the central atom that occupy space and repel other electron pairs, but are not included in molecular geometry names.
components.interpretation: Lone pairs compress bond angles and preferentially occupy positions that minimize repulsion (equatorial for SN=5, opposite for SN=6).

image.alt: Diagram comparing bond angles in CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O showing lone pair compression effect
image.caption: Lone pairs compress bond angles: CH₄ (0 LP, 109.5°) > NH₃ (1 LP, 107°) > H₂O (2 LP, 104.5°)

principles.statement: Lone pair-lone pair repulsion is strongest, followed by lone pair-bonding pair, then bonding pair-bonding pair repulsion.
principles.application: Explains why bond angles decrease as lone pair count increases; predicts which positions lone pairs occupy.
principles.analogy: Like different sized balloons: larger balloons (lone pairs) push smaller balloons (bonding pairs) closer together.

principles.statement: For trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry (SN=5), lone pairs preferentially occupy equatorial positions.
principles.application: Minimizes 90° LP-LP and LP-BP interactions; equatorial positions have only 2 × 90° interactions vs. 3 × 90° for axial.
principles.example: SF₄: lone pair occupies equatorial position → seesaw molecular geometry
principles.analogy: Like choosing a seat at a table: equatorial seats have fewer close neighbors than axial seats.

principles.statement: For octahedral electron domain geometry (SN=6), multiple lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions.
principles.application: Minimizes LP-LP repulsion by maximizing separation (180° apart); explains square planar geometry for SN=6 with 2 LP.
principles.example: XeF₄: 2 lone pairs occupy opposite positions → square planar molecular geometry
principles.analogy: Like sitting at opposite ends of a table to maximize distance from each other.

classifications.value: Bond angles systematically decrease as lone pair count increases
characteristics: SN = 4 family: CH₄ (0 LP) 109.5° > NH₃ (1 LP) 107° > H₂O (2 LP) 104.5°
Each lone pair compresses angles by ~2-2.5°
classifications.behavior: More lone pairs = greater bond angle compression from ideal values

classifications.value: Lone pairs occupy equatorial positions in trigonal bipyramidal geometry
characteristics: Lone pairs minimize repulsion by choosing equatorial
classifications.behavior: 1 LP → seesaw; 2 LP → T-shaped; 3 LP → linear

examples.explanation: Lone pair equatorial → seesaw geometry
Both lone pairs equatorial → T-shaped geometry
All three lone pairs equatorial → linear geometry

classifications.value: Lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions in octahedral geometry
classifications.behavior: 1 LP → square pyramidal; 2 LP → square planar

examples.explanation: One lone pair → square pyramidal geometry
Two lone pairs opposite → square planar geometry

worked_examples.title: Explaining Bond Angle Variations
worked_examples.problem: Explain why the bond angle in H₂S (92°) is smaller than in H₂O (104.5°), even though both have the same electron domain geometry.

step_by_step.step: Determine electron domain geometry for both
step_by_step.explanation: Both H₂O and H₂S have 4 electron domains (2 BP, 2 LP) → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
O is more electronegative (3.44) than S (2.58)
More electronegative O pulls bonding electrons closer to itself, away from central atom; bonding pairs occupy less space near central atom
step_by_step.calculation: H₂O: bonding pairs closer to O; H₂S: bonding pairs closer to S

step_by_step.explanation: In H₂S, bonding pairs are farther from central atom, so lone pairs exert relatively greater repulsion; bond angles compress more
step_by_step.calculation: H₂S: greater LP-BP repulsion effect → smaller angle

step_by_step.explanation: S is larger than O; bonding pairs are farther from nucleus and from each other; lone pair repulsion dominates more
step_by_step.calculation: Larger central atom → smaller bond angles with same lone pair count

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂S has smaller bond angle (92°) than H₂O (104.5°) because S is less electronegative and larger than O; bonding pairs are farther from central atom in H₂S, allowing lone pairs to compress bond angles more
worked_examples.concept_applied: Central atom electronegativity and size affect bond angles in addition to lone pair count; less electronegative, larger central atoms show greater angle compression.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Lone Pair Positions
worked_examples.problem: Explain why the lone pair in SF₄ occupies an equatorial position rather than an axial position in the trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry.

step_by_step.explanation: 5 electron domains: 3 equatorial (120° apart) + 2 axial (90° to equatorial)
Axial position has 3 × 90° interactions with equatorial domains
Equatorial position has 2 × 90° interactions with axial domains
Equatorial position has fewer 90° interactions (2 vs. 3); less repulsion
Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize 90° interactions to reduce repulsion

worked_examples.final_answer: Lone pair in SF₄ occupies equatorial position because it has only 2 × 90° interactions vs. 3 × 90° for axial position; minimizes LP-BP repulsion
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize 90° interactions; equatorial positions in trigonal bipyramidal have fewer 90° interactions than axial.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about VSEPR theory can lead to incorrect geometry predictions. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of molecular shape prediction.

classifications.type: Error: Confusing Electron Domain and Molecular Geometry
classifications.value: Using electron domain geometry name when molecular geometry is different due to lone pairs
characteristics: Calling NH₃ tetrahedral instead of trigonal pyramidal
Calling H₂O tetrahedral instead of bent
Not distinguishing between electron pair arrangement and atom arrangement
classifications.behavior: Remember: electron domain geometry includes lone pairs; molecular geometry names only atom positions

examples.explanation: Wrong: tetrahedral; Correct: electron domain = tetrahedral, molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal
Wrong: tetrahedral; Correct: electron domain = tetrahedral, molecular geometry = bent

classifications.value: Counting each bond in a multiple bond as separate electron domains
classifications.behavior: Remember: each bonding region (single, double, or triple bond) counts as ONE electron domain

examples.explanation: Wrong: 4 domains (counting each bond in double bonds); Correct: 2 domains (2 double bonds = 2 domains)
Wrong: 4 domains; Correct: 3 domains (2 single bonds + 1 double bond = 3 domains)

classifications.type: Error: Ignoring Lone Pair Effects on Bond Angles
classifications.value: Assuming bond angles always equal ideal values regardless of lone pairs
characteristics: Not accounting for lone pair compression
classifications.behavior: Remember: lone pairs compress bond angles; 1 LP ≈ 2-2.5° compression; 2 LP ≈ 5° compression

examples.explanation: Wrong: 109.5°; Correct: ~107° (1 lone pair compression)
Wrong: 109.5°; Correct: ~104.5° (2 lone pair compression)

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Lone Pair Positions for SN = 5 and 6
classifications.value: Placing lone pairs in positions that don't minimize repulsion
characteristics: Putting lone pairs in axial positions for SN = 5
Putting 2 lone pairs adjacent for SN = 6
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 5 lone pairs go equatorial; SN = 6 lone pairs go opposite (trans)

examples.explanation: Wrong: lone pair axial; Correct: lone pair equatorial → seesaw geometry
Wrong: lone pairs adjacent; Correct: lone pairs opposite → square planar geometry

classifications.type: Error: Forgetting to Draw Lewis Structure First
classifications.value: Attempting VSEPR without first determining correct Lewis structure
classifications.behavior: Always draw Lewis structure first to correctly count bonding pairs and lone pairs on central atom

examples.explanation: Without Lewis structure: might miss lone pair on S; With Lewis structure: correctly identify 2 BP + 1 LP

sections.introduction: Test your understanding of VSEPR theory with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of electron domain counting, geometry prediction, and lone pair effects.
key_concept.context: Focus on systematic approach: Lewis structure → electron domains → electron domain geometry → molecular geometry → bond angles.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Predict the electron domain geometry, molecular geometry, and bond angles for PCl₃.

step_by_step.explanation: P is central atom with 5 valence electrons; 3 Cl atoms bond; P has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: P: 5 valence e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 LP

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 4 electron domains
4 electron domains → tetrahedral
3 atoms + 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal
1 lone pair compresses from 109.5° to ~107°

worked_examples.final_answer: PCl₃: Electron domain geometry = tetrahedral; Molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal; Bond angle ≈ 107°
worked_examples.concept_applied: SN = 4 with 1 lone pair gives trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry (same as NH₃).

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for CO₃²⁻ (carbonate ion).

step_by_step.explanation: C is central atom with 4 valence electrons; 3 O atoms bond; -2 charge means 2 extra electrons; C has no lone pairs (uses all for bonding including one double bond)
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 valence e⁻ + 2 (charge) = 6 e⁻ for bonding; 3 bonding regions

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding regions (1 double bond + 2 single bonds count as 3 domains); 0 lone pairs
3 electron domains → trigonal planar
3 atoms + 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar
No lone pairs; ideal 120° angles

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₃²⁻: Electron domain geometry = trigonal planar; Molecular geometry = trigonal planar; Bond angle = 120°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple bonds count as single electron domains; 3 domains with 0 lone pairs = trigonal planar.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Which has larger bond angles: CH₄ or NH₃? Explain.

step_by_step.step: Determine electron domain geometry for both
step_by_step.explanation: Both have 4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
CH₄: 0 lone pairs; NH₃: 1 lone pair
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄: 4 BP, 0 LP; NH₃: 3 BP, 1 LP

step_by_step.explanation: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing bond angles
CH₄: 109.5° (ideal); NH₃: ~107° (compressed by lone pair)

worked_examples.final_answer: CH₄ has larger bond angles (109.5°) than NH₃ (~107°) because NH₃ has a lone pair that compresses bond angles
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs compress bond angles; molecules with more lone pairs have smaller bond angles for same steric number.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for ICl₄⁻.

step_by_step.explanation: I is central atom with 7 valence electrons; 4 Cl atoms bond; -1 charge means 1 extra electron; I has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: I: 7 valence e⁻ + 1 (charge) = 8 e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 LP

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron domains
6 electron domains → octahedral
2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions to minimize LP-LP repulsion
4 atoms in plane with 2 lone pairs above and below → square planar

worked_examples.final_answer: ICl₄⁻: Steric number = 6; Electron domain geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = square planar; Bond angle = 90°
worked_examples.concept_applied: SN = 6 with 2 lone pairs gives square planar molecular geometry; lone pairs occupy opposite positions.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Explain why CO₂ is linear but SO₂ is bent, even though both have a central atom bonded to two oxygen atoms.

step_by_step.explanation: C is central with 4 valence electrons; forms 2 double bonds with O; no lone pairs on C
S is central with 6 valence electrons; forms bonds with 2 O; has 1 lone pair on S
CO₂: 2 electron domains; SO₂: 3 electron domains
CO₂: SN = 2 → linear; SO₂: SN = 3 → bent (due to lone pair)
Different central atoms have different valence electron counts, leading to different lone pair counts and geometries
step_by_step.calculation: Central atom identity matters for VSEPR predictions

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂ is linear (SN = 2, 0 LP) because C has no lone pairs; SO₂ is bent (SN = 3, 1 LP) because S has a lone pair that affects geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Central atom valence electron count determines lone pair count; different central atoms with same bonding pattern can have different geometries.

summary: VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry based on electron pair repulsion around a central atom. Key principles: (1) Electron pairs (bonding + lone pairs) arrange to maximize separation; (2) Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs (LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP); (3) Multiple bonds count as single electron domains but exert greater repulsion than single bonds. Steric number (SN) = bonding pairs + lone pairs determines electron domain geometry: SN = 2 (linear, 180°), SN = 3 (trigonal planar, 120°), SN = 4 (tetrahedral, 109.5°), SN = 5 (trigonal bipyramidal, 90°/120°), SN = 6 (octahedral, 90°). Molecular geometry depends on atom positions only: SN = 4 with 0 LP = tetrahedral, 1 LP = trigonal pyramidal, 2 LP = bent. Lone pairs compress bond angles (CH₄ 109.5° > NH₃ 107° > H₂O 104.5°). For SN = 5, lone pairs occupy equatorial positions; for SN = 6, lone pairs occupy opposite positions. Common errors include: confusing electron domain and molecular geometry, miscounting electron domains (multiple bonds = 1 domain), ignoring lone pair angle compression, and incorrect lone pair positions. Systematic approach: Lewis structure → count electron domains → determine electron domain geometry → determine molecular geometry → predict bond angles. Mastery of VSEPR enables prediction of molecular shapes, polarity, and reactivity for main group compounds.
